The Repetition That Dwells Within the BodyThe prison is not made of walls; it is a body that repeats itself with astonishing devotion.
Every morning, the same fingers awaken — the same number, the same length, the same curves. The eyes open to the same scene, the ears receive the world in the same manner, and the heart insists on beating as if it has not grown tired of performing the same task since birth. Even the skin knows nothing but how to remain itself, covering the bones with the same silence and aging in the same inevitable way.
How many thousands of days does a human being need before realizing that they are living inside a machine that knows only repetition?
We do not inhabit our bodies; our bodies inhabit us. They are the only tyrant we never chose, yet we obey them until our final breath. We cannot replace an eye that has grown weary of seeing, a hand that has grown tired of holding, or a heart exhausted by its own beating. The body is not a possession; it is a destiny wearing your name.
The strange thing is that humans believe they are searching for the new, while carrying with them the oldest thing in existence: this body that, since its birth, has done nothing but replay the same scene. An inhale is an inhale, an exhale is an exhale, a step is a step, and a blink is a blink. We do not live time; we repeat movements until time itself dissolves from excessive repetition.
Perhaps this is why humans invented travel, love, wars, religions, arts, drugs, and every form of noise. They were not searching for happiness, but for a small crack in the wall of repetition. Yet the body mocks them all. It returns every evening to demand food, then sleep, then awakens to begin another version of the previous day, as if everything we have done was nothing more than a brief pause between two repetitions.
The body is a statue made of flesh.
Not because it is worshipped, but because it imposes its rituals upon us every day. We become hungry when it commands, thirsty when it commands, tired when it commands, and we die when it decides. Even the most rebellious minds eventually surrender before a small headache or a toothache. A single inflamed nerve is enough to defeat the philosophy of an entire lifetime.
How much humans speak of freedom, and how little of it they truly possess. Their first jailer was their skeleton, their first law was the rhythm of their heartbeat, and their first chain was their own skin.
The most terrifying thing is not that the body will perish, but that it repeats itself without mercy until the moment of its destruction. It does not grant us a grand ending; it kills us through familiarity. It makes life appear so ordinary that we stop noticing it. Then, when it finally collapses, we realize that everything we lived was nothing but a long ritual preparing us for death.
Perhaps death is not the opposite of life.
Perhaps death is the first moment when this repetition finally comes to an end.
BY ✍🏻 Rose AlMuhessen
rosekma214@gmail.com
